---
execute:
  eval: false
---

# Load a repo into Foundry

## Objective

Bring an existing GitHub repository into a Foundry **Code Repository** so you can run it in a Foundry **Code Workspace** VS Code session to document your work inside the Foundry enclave.

This chapter is a prerequisite for:

- `fnd-threads.qmd` (Initiate a reproducible Threads workflow)

## Prerequisites

Before you start, complete:

- `fnd-getting-started.qmd` (login, create a project, tagging for discovery, groups/permissions, etc.)

You also need:

- access to create a Code Repository in your Foundry Project
- access to the GitHub repository you will import

## Which repo should I import?

For USACE data science work, we recommend starting from the approved lightweight Python analysis template described in:

- `repo-create-generic-python-repo.qmd`

That chapter’s workflow creates a new GitHub repository based on:

- Template repo: `MVR-GIS/repo-generic-python`

In Foundry, you will typically import **your new repo** (created from the template), not the template itself.

Why this matters:

- the template includes the standard project structure (`notebooks/`, `src/`, `outputs/`, etc.)
- it also includes repository-local tooling that downstream chapters rely on (example: the Threads export extractor in `tools/`)

## Key steps (overview)

1. Create your analysis repo in GitHub from the approved template (external step).
3. Create an empty Foundry Code Repository.
4. 

## Initial Configuration
Follow these steps once to ensure you have a working environment. 

### Install DoD Certificates
`InstalRoot` is the app that DoD uses to ensure secure communication. 
- Use USACE App Portal to install `InstallRoot`.
- Open the `InstallRoot` app and click the "Install Certificates" button to install the required certificates. 

### Tell Git to use the Windows credential store

```bash
git config --global http.sslBackend schannel
```

### Install the Palantir extension for Visual Studio Code
The Palantir extension for VS Code is used to clone a repository stored in Foundry Code Repositories. See [install instructions here.](https://vantage.army.mil/docs/foundry/palantir-extension-for-visual-studio-code/overview/) 
- Download the extension `.visx` file.
- On the VS Code Extensions tab, use the "three dot" menu, and select "Install from VSIX..."
- In the Open dialog, navigate to the palantir extension `.vsix` file and click the "Install" button. 

---

## Step 1 — In Githib, Create your analysis repo

- Follow the instructions: `repo-create-generic-python-repo.qmd`
- When you’re done, you should have a GitHub repository you own/control (example: `<OWNER>/<my-analysis-project>`).

## Step 2 - In VS Code on you computer, clone this repo locally

- Open VS Code on your computer. 
- Copy the Repository URL you created in the previous step: Go to your repository (e.g., on GitHub) and copy the clone URL.
- In VS Code, open the Command Palette: (Press Ctrl + Shift + P). Run the Clone Command: Type `Git: Clone` and select it from the list. 
- Paste the repo URL: Enter the repository URL at GitHub when prompted and press Enter. 
- Select a Destination: A file explorer window will open. Select or create the parent folder where you want the new repository folder to live. VS Code will automatically create a new subfolder named after the repository inside this location. Choose to open in a new session. 
- Or, in a Git bash terminal use the following command: 

```bash
git clone https://github.com/<OWNER>/<REPO> <PARENT-FOLDER-NAME>
```

- You will now see your newly cloned repo in your VS Code session running on your computer. 

## Step 3 - In Palantir Foundry Code Repositories, create a new empty repository

- Open a web browser and log into Army Vantage (Palantir Foundry). 
- Go to the `Code Repositories` platform app. 
- Create a new empty repository. Give it the same name as the repo you just created in Github. 
- Click the "Work Locally" button and copy the URL to the repository. 
- This <foundry-repo-url> should be structured something like: `https://<your-id>:<giant-string-1>@vantage.army.mil/stemma/git/ri.stemma.main.repository.<giant-string-2>/<your-repo-name>`

## Step 4 - In VS Code on your computer, add Foundry as a remote

- In the local version of your repo, we need to add the Palantir Foundry Code Repository repo you created in the last step as a `remote`. 
- Use the following `git` command to add your Foundry repo as a `remote`. Replace the <foundry-repo-url> with the URL you copied from Foundry in the last step. 

```bash
git remote add foundry <foundry-repo-url>
```

- Use the `git remote -v` command to check that the `remote` was added correctly. 

```bash
git remote -v
foundry https://<your-id>:<giant-string-1>@@vantage.army.mil/stemma/git/ri.stemma.main.repository.<giant-string-2>/<your-repo-name> (fetch)
foundry https://<your-id>:<giant-string-1>@@vantage.army.mil/stemma/git/ri.stemma.main.repository.<giant-string-2>/<your-repo-name> (push)
origin  https://github.com/<OWNER>/<your-repo-name>.git (fetch)
origin  https://github.com/<OWNER>/<your-repo-name>.git (push)
```

- Push your local branches (e.g., main) to the Foundry remote:

```bash
git push foundry main
```

- If you have branches other than `main`, push them as well.

## Step 5 - Verify in Foundry

- Open the new Code Repository in Foundry to confirm your code and history have been imported.

Required outcome:

- The Code Repository file tree inside Foundry matches the GitHub repository contents at the commit you downloaded.

## Step 6 — Verify repository structure and tooling (inside Foundry Code Workspace)

This verification step must occur inside the **Foundry Code Workspace** UI for the Code Repository you just imported.

### 6.1 Open the repo in Code Workspace

1. In Foundry, open your Project.
2. Open the Code Repository you imported.
3. Open it in **Code Workspace** so you can browse files and run commands (if enabled in your environment).

### 6.2 Confirm the expected files/folders exist

At minimum, verify you see the expected top-level folders/files, such as:

- `notebooks/`
- `src/`
- `data/` *(if your policy allows storing local data in-repo; often data should be in Foundry Datasets instead)*
- `outputs/`
- `README.md`
- `environment.yml`

Also verify the presence of the Threads workflow tooling (required for `fnd-threads.qmd`):

- `tools/`
  - `foundry_threads.py`
  - `__init__.py` *(so `python -m tools.foundry_threads` works)*
- `pytest.ini` *(supports consistent test imports)*
- `tests/` *(if your repo includes tests; recommended)*

### 6.3 Quick “tool exists” sanity check

If your Foundry Code Workspace environment supports running Python in a terminal:

From the repository root, run:

```bash
python -m tools.foundry_threads --help
```

Expected outcome:

- Help text prints without import errors (confirms the repo-local tool is present and importable)

If you cannot run a terminal in Code Workspace (or Python execution is constrained), you can still verify:

- the file tree includes the required tool files listed above
- the `README.md` documents how the tool should be run in your environment

Recommendations:

- Do not rely on `pip install ...` or `conda env create ...` fetching packages from the internet during normal execution.
- Prefer platform-provided Python environments or curated package sets where available.
- If dependency installation is required, coordinate with your Foundry administrators on the approved mechanism (mirrors, curated channels, base images, etc.).

## Definition of done

You are ready to proceed when:

- the GitHub repo has been imported into a Foundry Code Repository
- you can browse the repo file tree in Foundry Code Workspace
- you recorded provenance:
  - GitHub repo URL
  - commit SHA (or tag/release) corresponding to the push you imported
- permissions/groups are set so collaborators can access the Code Repo (as intended)
- (recommended) you confirmed the Threads tool is present:
  - `python -m tools.foundry_threads --help` runs successfully, or the required files exist in the repo tree

---

## Push changes out of Foundry to local repo

- Fetch changes from Foundry - This retrieves all branches and updates from Foundry:

```bash
git fetch foundry
```

- Pull Changes to your local branch - Update your local branch (e.g., main) with changes from Foundry:

```bash
git pull foundry main
```

- Repeat for other branches by replacing `main` with the branch you want to update.
- Resolve any merge conflicts - If there are conflicts, Git will prompt you to resolve them locally.
